culturalTraits: Built powerful Mossi kingdoms (1200s-1896) that resisted Mali and Songhai
Distinctive Nakomsé (royal) and Nyonyosé (common) class system
Elaborate court protocols around the Mogho Naaba (emperor)
Weekly audiences with the Mogho Naaba still held in Ouagadougou
funFacts: The Mossi Kingdoms successfully resisted the Mali and Songhai Empires
The Mogho Naaba (emperor) still holds weekly public audiences in Ouagadougou
Mossi make up over half of Burkina Faso's population
Mossi labor migrants are found across West Africa and beyond

historical: Internal trade in grain, cotton, livestock
modern: Labor migration to Côte d'Ivoire and Ghana

independenceHistory.antiColonialResistance: Mogho Naaba Wobgho resisted French conquest in 1896, going into exile rather than submit. Mossi soldiers fought in French colonial forces.
independenceHistory.independenceMovement: Maurice Yaméogo (first president) and Thomas Sankara (revolutionary leader) had Mossi heritage. Sankara renamed country 'Burkina Faso' (Land of Upright People).

traditionalFood.description: Mossi cuisine features tô (thick millet paste) dipped in vegetable and meat sauces. Dolo millet beer is central to social gatherings.

tribes.traditionalDance: Warba - warrior dance with elaborate costumes

traditionalReligion.name: Mossi Traditional Religion
traditionalReligion.supremeDeity: Wende (supreme god, creator)
spirits: Nature spirits of trees and waters
practices: Royal rituals at the Mogho Naaba court
traditionalReligion.beliefs: Ancestors directly influence fortune; earth priests maintain relationship with land spirits; the Mogho Naaba has spiritual as well as political authority; Mossi resisted Islamization longer than neighbors, maintaining traditional practices

tribes.description: The Mossi are Burkina Faso's dominant ethnic group, heirs to powerful kingdoms that resisted Mali and Songhai empires, with a living emperor (Mogho Naaba) who still holds weekly audiences.

tribes.name: Dagomba (Dagbamba)
culturalTraits: Famous Lunsi (talking) drummers who preserve oral history
Ya Na (king) paramount chief system still active
funFacts: Lunsi drummers can recite centuries of royal genealogy from memory
The Dagbon kingdom dates back to the 15th century
Damba festival celebrates the Prophet's birthday with fire dances
Smock weaving is a highly valued craft

historical: Traded slaves, gold, kola nuts northward
modern: Rice and grain production for Ghana

traditionalFood.description: Dagomba cuisine centers on TZ (tuo zaafi), a thick millet paste served with soups. Guinea fowl is a prized protein.

tribes.traditionalDance: Damba - fire festival dance with elaborate drumming
tribes.description: The Dagomba of northern Ghana are known for the 500-year-old Dagbon Kingdom, Lunsi talking drummers who preserve oral history, and elaborate fire festivals.

tribes.name: Mangbetu
culturalTraits: Famous Lipombo tradition of head elongation (now discontinued)
Sophisticated royal court with elaborate etiquette
funFacts: The Mangbetu skull elongation tradition inspired European artists and fashion
Their king's court rivaled many European palaces in sophistication
Mangbetu harps and other instruments are prized museum pieces
Women's elaborate hairstyles could take days to create

historical: Supplied enslaved people during slave trade era

traditionalFood.description: Mangbetu cuisine features forest products, cassava, and plantain. Palm wine is central to social gatherings.

tribes.traditionalDance: Royal court dances with elaborate costumes and music
tribes.description: The Mangbetu of northeastern DRC are known for their former Lipombo head elongation tradition, sophisticated royal court culture, distinctive hairstyles, and refined artistic heritage.

tribes.name: Ovimbundu
culturalTraits: Historically powerful traders linking coast to interior
funFacts: The Ovimbundu are Angola's largest ethnic group at 37%
They built trading networks spanning thousands of kilometers
Jonas Savimbi (UNITA leader) was Ovimbundu
Their plateau homeland was a refuge from slave raiders

historical: Controlled major trade routes to Atlantic coast
Acted as middlemen between interior and Portuguese

independenceHistory.antiColonialResistance: The Ovimbundu initially traded with Portuguese but later resisted expansion. During civil war, UNITA (largely Ovimbundu) fought MPLA government.
independenceHistory.independenceMovement: Jonas Savimbi founded UNITA in 1966. The 27-year civil war (1975-2002) devastated Ovimbundu heartland.

traditionalFood.description: Ovimbundu cuisine features highland crops like maize and beans. The temperate climate allows diverse agriculture.

tribes.traditionalDance: Initiation dances and harvest celebrations
tribes.religion: Christianity (Catholic and Protestant majority), Traditional beliefs
tribes.description: The Ovimbundu are Angola's largest ethnic group, historically powerful traders who controlled routes from the interior to the Atlantic coast, now known for highland agriculture.

tribes.name: Chokwe
culturalTraits: Pwo (female) and Mwana Pwo masks are iconic
funFacts: Chokwe masks are among the most valuable African art in world museums
The Mwana Pwo mask represents the ideal woman
Chokwe territory includes major diamond deposits
They conquered the Lunda Empire in the 19th century

historical: Controlled routes between Angola and Congo

traditionalFood.description: Chokwe cuisine reflects their hunter heritage with game meats complementing cassava staples.

tribes.traditionalDance: Mukanda initiation dances with elaborate masks
tribes.description: The Chokwe are master mask carvers spanning Angola, DRC, and Zambia, known for their Pwo and Mwana Pwo masks that represent ideals of beauty and are prized in world museums.

tribes.name: Sara
culturalTraits: Resistance to forced labor under French rule
funFacts: Sara people were conscripted as soldiers by France and fought in both World Wars
Traditional Sara houses are round mud structures with thatched roofs
Sara wrestling festivals draw thousands of spectators
Many Sara converted to Christianity resisting Islam from the north

traditionalFood.description: Sara cuisine centers on millet and sorghum with fish from the Chari and Logone rivers.

tribes.traditionalDance: Sara wrestling festivals with ceremonial dances
tribes.antiColonialHistory: Sara people resisted French forced labor and conscription policies throughout the colonial period. Despite exploitation, Sara soldiers fought for France in WWI and WWII.
tribes.description: The Sara are Chad's largest ethnic group, known for traditional wrestling, scarification, and cotton farming, with a history of French colonial exploitation and military service.

tribes.name: Toubou (Tubu)
culturalTraits: Master navigators of the Sahara desert
Divided into Teda (north) and Daza (south) subgroups
Known for endurance in extreme heat
Portable tent dwellings for nomadic life
funFacts: Toubou can survive on just a handful of dates and camel milk for days
They were among the last groups to resist French colonial control
Two Chadian presidents (Goukouni and Habré) are Toubou
Toubou women have significant property rights and autonomy

traditionalFood.description: Toubou diet is adapted to desert scarcity, relying heavily on preserved foods and camel products.

tribes.traditionalDance: Sword dances and warrior celebrations
tribes.antiColonialHistory: The Toubou fiercely resisted French colonization, with the Tibesti region remaining largely ungovernable until the 1930s. They continue to assert autonomy in border regions.
tribes.description: The Toubou are legendary Saharan nomads spanning Chad, Libya, Niger, and Sudan, known as some of the toughest desert survivors on Earth with a fierce warrior tradition.

tribes.name: Zarma (Djerma)
culturalTraits: Closely related to Songhai people linguistically
Historically migrated from Mali's inland delta
Traditional fishing and farming along Niger River
Joking relationships (cousinage) with other ethnic groups
funFacts: Zarma and Songhai consider each other cousins and can intermarry freely
Most of Niger's post-independence presidents have been Zarma
Zarma language is a major lingua franca in southwestern Niger
Traditional Zarma society had elaborate caste systems

historical: Part of Songhai Empire trade networks
modern: Cross-border trade with Nigeria and Benin

traditionalFood.description: Zarma cuisine benefits from the Niger River, combining millet staples with fresh fish and vegetables.

tribes.traditionalDance: Zarma spirit possession ceremonies and harvest dances
tribes.description: The Zarma are Niger's second largest ethnic group after the Hausa, closely related to the Songhai, and dominant in the capital Niamey with a strong political presence.

tribes.name: Moors (Beidane)
funFacts: Mauritanian tea ceremony requires three glasses: bitter like death, sweet like life, gentle like love
The ancient libraries of Chinguetti hold manuscripts from the 12th century
Moorish epic poetry (T'heydinn) is UNESCO-protected heritage
Mauritania was the last country to officially abolish slavery in 1981

traditionalFood.description: Moorish cuisine blends Saharan nomadic traditions with Atlantic coastal influences.

tribes.traditionalDance: Traditional griot performances with tidinit lute
tribes.description: The Moors (Beidane) are the dominant Arab-Berber ethnic group of Mauritania, known for Hassaniya Arabic, desert nomadism, ancient manuscript libraries, and elaborate tea ceremonies.

tribes.name: Soninke
culturalTraits: Long tradition of labor migration to France
Caste system with griots and blacksmiths
funFacts: The Soninke founded the Ghana Empire (4th-13th centuries), the first great West African empire
Soninke diaspora sends billions in remittances annually
The Wagadu legend tells of a serpent that protected the empire in exchange for virgin sacrifices
Soninke from Mali and Senegal have large communities in Paris and New York

historical: Ghana Empire was a trading superpower

traditionalFood.description: Soninke cuisine shares West African Sahelian traditions with emphasis on grains and peanut-based dishes.

tribes.traditionalDance: Didadi harvest celebrations
tribes.antiColonialHistory: Soninke warriors resisted French colonization, notably during the resistance led by Mahmadou Lamine Dramé in the 1880s against French expansion in Senegal.
tribes.description: The Soninke are the founders of the ancient Ghana Empire, now spread across the Sahel with a strong diaspora tradition and reputation for entrepreneurship and migration.

tribes.name: Cape Verdean (Crioulo)
culturalTraits: Unique Creole culture blending African and Portuguese
More Cape Verdeans live abroad than on the islands
Each island has distinct identity and dialect
funFacts: More Cape Verdeans live in the diaspora (USA, Portugal, France) than in Cape Verde itself
Cesária Évora, the 'Barefoot Diva', made morna music world-famous
Morna music is inscribed on UNESCO's Intangible Cultural Heritage list (2019)
Cape Verde was uninhabited before Portuguese colonization in the 1460s

specialDishes: Cachupa (national dish - corn and bean stew)
traditionalFood.description: Cape Verdean cuisine centers on cachupa, a hearty corn and bean stew, with abundant fresh seafood.

tribes.traditionalDance: Morna (slow, melancholic) and Coladeira (upbeat)
tribes.description: Cape Verdeans are a Creole people of the Atlantic islands, known worldwide for morna music, a culture of saudade (longing), and one of Africa's largest diaspora communities.

tribes.name: Comorian
culturalTraits: Grand Marriage (Anda) is central social institution
Strong Arab, African, and Malagasy cultural blend
funFacts: Comoros is called the 'Perfume Islands' due to ylang-ylang and vanilla production
Grand Marriage ceremonies can cost more than a house and take years to save for
Comorians have close ties to Swahili coast cultures
The coelacanth, a 'living fossil' fish, was rediscovered off Comoros in 1938

specialDishes: Langouste à la vanille (lobster with vanilla)
traditionalFood.description: Comorian cuisine reflects Indian Ocean influences with coconut, spices, and fresh seafood.

tribes.traditionalDance: Twarab (influenced by Arabic music) and traditional dances
tribes.description: Comorians are the people of the Comoros Islands, a unique blend of Arab, African, and Malagasy cultures, known for elaborate Grand Marriages, perfume exports, and Swahili heritage.

tribes.name: Nubian

traditional.male: Men farmed date palms, fished the Nile, and built the iconic Nubian houses. Wrestling (Nuba wrestling) was a prestigious sport.
traditional.female: Women managed households, created distinctive Nubian decorations, and were renowned for henna artistry and pottery.

modern.male: Many Nubian men work in tourism, construction, and urban professions. Many were displaced by the Aswan Dam.
modern.female: Nubian women are educators, preservationists, and advocates for the return to ancestral lands.

culturalTraits: Builders of over 200 pyramids at Meroë
Iconic Nubian music and ariti songs
funFacts: Nubia has more pyramids (~255) than Egypt (~138)
The Kingdom of Kush conquered and ruled Egypt for nearly 100 years (25th Dynasty)
Nubian wrestlers have a tradition dating back 3,000 years
Many Nubians were displaced by the Aswan High Dam in the 1960s and seek to return
Nubian houses are famous for their bright colors and geometric designs

historical: Gold from Nubian mines ('Nubia' may derive from 'nub' meaning gold)
Incense and ivory trade with Egypt

tribes.antiColonialResistance: Nubians resisted Ottoman, Egyptian, and British rule. The Mahdist revolution (1881-1898) had Nubian participants fighting for independence.

traditionalFood.description: Nubian cuisine features Nile fish, dates, sorghum bread, and the famous hibiscus tea served to all guests.

tribes.traditionalDance: Ariti (traditional Nubian singing and dance with drums)
tribes.religion: Islam (Sunni), with ancient Nubian Christian heritage

history.origin: Nubians are one of the oldest civilizations in Africa, with kingdoms dating back 5,000 years. The Kingdom of Kush, Meroë, and Nobatia were powerful Nubian states.
history.colonialEra: Nubia was colonized by Egypt, then the Anglo-Egyptian Condominium. The Aswan Dam (1902, 1960s) flooded ancestral lands.
history.independence: Nubians in Sudan have sought greater recognition and the right to return to lands flooded by Lake Nasser/Nubia.

language.name: Nobiin (and Kenzi-Dongolawi)
language.writingSystem: Arabic script, Old Nubian script historically

tribes.description: The Nubians are one of Africa's oldest civilizations, builders of the pyramids of Meroë, rulers of Egypt's 25th Dynasty, and guardians of a rich cultural heritage along the Nile.

tribes.name: Beja

traditional.male: Men herded camels, defended territory, and were renowned for sword fighting. The Hadendoa were called 'Fuzzy-Wuzzies' by British soldiers for their distinctive hair.
traditional.female: Women managed camps, processed milk products, wove mats, and dismantled/rebuilt tents during migrations.

modern.male: Many Beja men work in Port Sudan, gold mining, or remain in pastoralism. Some were affected by Eastern Sudan conflicts.
modern.female: Beja women are increasingly in education while maintaining pastoral traditions.

culturalTraits: Nomadic pastoralist lifestyle in harsh deserts
Famous Hadendoa warriors who fought the British
Camels are central to life and bride-price
funFacts: The Beja's Hadendoa warriors fought ferociously against British forces and inspired Kipling's poem 'Fuzzy-Wuzzy'
Beja have maintained their language and culture for over 4,000 years
The Beja controlled trade routes from the Nile to the Red Sea for millennia
Ancient Egyptians traded with Beja ancestors for gold and incense
Some historians believe Queen of Sheba had Beja connections
tribes.antiColonialResistance: The Hadendoa Beja were fierce fighters against British colonialism. At the Battle of Tamai (1884), they broke British infantry squares, earning respect as fearsome warriors.

traditionalFood.description: Beja diet centers on camel and goat products, suited to their desert nomadic lifestyle.

tribes.traditionalDance: Sword dances and warrior performances

history.origin: The Beja are one of the oldest peoples in Africa, mentioned by ancient Egyptians, Greeks (as Blemmyes), and Romans. They've lived in the Eastern Desert for over 4,000 years.
history.colonialEra: The Beja resisted Egyptian, British, and Mahdist control. The Hadendoa fought in the Mahdist War against the British.
history.independence: Beja faced marginalization in Sudan, leading to Eastern Sudan conflicts and the Beja Congress political movement.

tribes.description: The Beja are ancient Cushitic nomads of the Eastern Sudan desert, renowned as fierce warriors (the 'Fuzzy-Wuzzies'), skilled camel herders, and keepers of a 4,000-year-old culture.

tribes.name: Dinka
tribes.region: South Sudan (Bahr el Ghazal, Upper Nile)

traditional.male: Men's lives revolved around cattle. Boys were initiated through scarification. Men defended herds and competed in wrestling.
traditional.female: Women farmed, milked cows, and managed households. Marriage required large cattle bride-prices (over 100 cattle for elite families).

modern.male: Civil war displaced many. Dinka men serve in government and military. Salva Kiir (President) and John Garang were Dinka.
modern.female: Women face challenges from conflict but are increasingly in education and advocacy.

culturalTraits: Cattle are central to life, wealth, and identity
Among the world's tallest people (average male height 6'4")
Oral poetry and songs about cattle
Wrestling is a major traditional sport
Nilotic pastoral lifestyle along White Nile
funFacts: Dinka cattle have names, and men compose songs praising their favorite cows
The Dinka are among the tallest people on Earth, with average male height around 6'4" (193 cm)
A Dinka man's wealth is measured in cattle - 100+ cows are needed for a prestigious marriage
Dinka have lived in South Sudan for over 1,000 years as pastoralists
John Garang, founder of South Sudan's independence movement, was Dinka
tribes.antiColonialResistance: Dinka resisted Arab slave raiders for centuries. During civil wars (1955-2005), Dinka formed the core of the SPLM/A under John Garang, fighting for South Sudan's independence.

traditionalFood.description: Dinka cuisine centers on cattle products (milk, blood, meat) and sorghum, reflecting their pastoral lifestyle.

tribes.traditionalDance: Jumping dances and cattle-themed songs
tribes.religion: Traditional Dinka religion (Nhialic - sky god), Christianity

history.origin: The Dinka migrated to their current lands along the White Nile around 1000 CE. They are part of the Nilotic peoples.
history.colonialEra: British rule (1898-1956) disrupted traditional life. Arab slave raids devastated Dinka communities before colonization.
history.independence: South Sudan gained independence in 2011. Civil war (2013-2020) heavily affected Dinka areas.

famousPeople.name: John Garang
famousPeople.role: SPLM/A leader, South Sudan founding father

tribes.description: The Dinka are the largest ethnic group in South Sudan, renowned as the world's tallest people, devoted cattle herders, and key figures in South Sudan's independence struggle.

tribes.name: Nuer

traditional.male: Men spent months in cattle camps along rivers. Boys were initiated with forehead scarification (gar). Men defended herds and raided.
traditional.female: Women farmed during the flood season, milked cows, and managed households. Bride-price was paid in cattle.

modern.male: Civil war displaced many. Riek Machar (Vice President) is Nuer. Many in the diaspora (USA, Australia).
modern.female: Nuer women face displacement but are increasingly vocal in peace movements.

culturalTraits: Cattle are sacred and central to identity
Seasonal migration between dry-season camps and wet-season villages
funFacts: The Nuer were the subject of a famous anthropology book 'The Nuer' by E.E. Evans-Pritchard
Nuer practice 'ghost marriage' where a dead man's relatives marry a woman to bear children in his name
They have no traditional chiefs - decisions are made by consensus among elders
Nuer and Dinka have fought cattle raids for centuries but share cultural similarities
Men in cattle camps sleep with their cattle and know each cow individually
tribes.antiColonialResistance: Nuer fiercely resisted British colonial rule. The Nuer prophet Ngundeng Bong led resistance, and his prophecies are still remembered. Nuer formed significant forces in South Sudan's independence war.

traditionalFood.description: Nuer diet relies heavily on cattle products and fish from the Nile rivers.

tribes.traditionalDance: Jumping dances, wrestling, and cattle songs
tribes.religion: Traditional Nuer religion (Kwoth - sky god), Christianity

history.origin: Nuer migrated into their current lands in the Sudd region over several centuries, expanding at the expense of other groups.
history.colonialEra: British 'pacification' campaigns attempted to end cattle raiding. Nuer resisted strongly.
history.independence: Nuer were key in the independence struggle. Post-2013 civil war divided Nuer and Dinka politically.

famousPeople.name: Emma McCune
famousPeople.role: Aid worker who married Riek Machar

tribes.description: The Nuer are a Nilotic people of South Sudan, famous for their cattle-centered culture, egalitarian society, and the iconic anthropological study by Evans-Pritchard.

tribes.name: Tigrinya

traditional.male: Men farmed the terraced highlands, served in the church, and defended territory. Literacy in Ge'ez was prestigious.
traditional.female: Women managed households, performed the coffee ceremony, and were skilled in traditional weaving and pottery.

modern.male: The 30-year independence war and conscription have shaped modern Eritrean men. Many in diaspora.
modern.female: Women were fighters in the independence struggle. Eritrea had significant female combatants.

culturalTraits: Use of Ge'ez script (one of Africa's oldest alphabets)
Coffee ceremony is a daily social ritual
Long independence struggle shaped national identity
funFacts: Tigrinya uses the Ge'ez script, one of the oldest alphabets still in use (2,500+ years)
The coffee ceremony is a 2-3 hour ritual performed multiple times daily
Eritrea's 30-year war for independence (1961-1991) was one of Africa's longest
About 30% of Eritrean independence fighters were women
Asmara's modernist architecture earned UNESCO World Heritage status in 2017
tribes.antiColonialResistance: Eritreans fought Italian colonization (1880s-1941), British administration, Ethiopian annexation, and a 30-year independence war (1961-1991) - one of Africa's most determined resistance movements.

traditionalFood.description: Tigrinya cuisine centers on injera topped with spiced stews (tsebhi). The coffee ceremony is a sacred social institution.

tribes.traditionalDance: Guayla (shoulder-shaking dance) and Kuda (jumping dance)

history.origin: Tigrinya-speaking peoples descend from the ancient Aksumite Empire, one of the great civilizations of antiquity.
history.colonialEra: Italian colonization (1890-1941), British administration, Ethiopian annexation (1962). The 30-year war for independence followed.
history.independence: Eritrea gained independence in 1993. The 1998-2000 border war with Ethiopia and recent Tigray conflict have marked recent history.

tribes.description: The Tigrinya are the largest ethnic group in Eritrea, heirs to the ancient Aksumite Empire, known for their Orthodox Christian faith, Ge'ez script, coffee ceremony, and hard-won independence.

tribes.name: Afar

traditional.male: Men herded camels, mined and traded salt, and defended territory. The curved dagger (gile) was a symbol of manhood.
traditional.female: Women built the oval huts (ari), managed water supplies, and processed milk products in the harsh desert environment.

modern.male: Salt mining remains important. Some work in ports (Djibouti, Assab). Climate change and conflict have affected pastoralism.
modern.female: Women face water scarcity challenges but maintain traditional roles. Some work in salt processing.

culturalTraits: Live in one of Earth's hottest regions (Danakil Depression)
The curved dagger (gile) is a male symbol
funFacts: The Afar live in the Danakil Depression, one of the hottest and lowest places on Earth
The region has active volcanoes, sulfur springs, and salt flats - an alien landscape
Afar salt miners still carve salt bars by hand and transport them by camel caravan
The Afar Triangle is where the human fossil 'Lucy' (3.2 million years old) was discovered
Afar warriors historically had a reputation so fierce that outsiders rarely entered their territory

historical: Salt trade with Ethiopian highlands (millennia old)
Caravan routes to Red Sea ports

tribes.antiColonialResistance: The Afar Sultanate of Aussa maintained independence for centuries. They resisted Ethiopian expansion, Italian colonization, and later marginalization. The Afar Liberation Front fought for regional autonomy.

traditionalFood.description: Afar diet is adapted to their harsh desert environment, relying on camel and goat products.

tribes.traditionalDance: Warrior dances with daggers

history.origin: The Afar have lived in the Horn of Africa for millennia, with possible ancestral links to Arabian Peninsula peoples.
history.colonialEra: The Sultanate of Aussa maintained autonomy. Italy colonized parts of Afar territory (Eritrea, Djibouti). Ethiopia incorporated Afar lands.
history.independence: Afar were divided between Eritrea, Ethiopia, and Djibouti at independence. They have sought greater autonomy in all three countries.

tribes.description: The Afar are a Cushitic people of the Horn of Africa's hottest region, known for salt mining, camel herding, fierce independence, and living in the otherworldly Danakil Depression.

tribes.name: Chewa

traditional.male: Men performed in Gule Wamkulu masked dances, fished Lake Malawi, and cleared land. Inheritance passed through the mother's line.
traditional.female: Women owned land and controlled family lineage in this matrilineal society. They were primary farmers and decision-makers.

modern.male: Men work in fishing, tobacco farming, and urban employment. Some Gule Wamkulu traditions continue.
modern.female: Women maintain strong roles in agriculture and family. Education has increased opportunities.

culturalTraits: Gule Wamkulu secret society and masked dances (UNESCO heritage)
funFacts: The Gule Wamkulu masked dances are performed by a secret society - women and children must not know the dancers' identities
Chewa society is matrilineal - children belong to the mother's clan and property passes through women
Lake Malawi, which borders Chewa lands, has more fish species than any other lake on Earth
The Chewa are the largest ethnic group in Malawi and gave the country its national language, Chichewa
Malawi means 'flames' in Chichewa, referring to the sun rising over Lake Malawi

modern: Cross-border trade with Zambia and Mozambique

tribes.antiColonialResistance: The Chewa resisted British colonial rule and forced labor. John Chilembwe led the 1915 uprising against colonial injustices. Hastings Kamuzu Banda, a Chewa, led Malawi to independence.

traditionalFood.description: Chewa cuisine centers on nsima (thick maize porridge) served with fish from Lake Malawi and vegetable relishes.

tribes.traditionalDance: Gule Wamkulu (masked dance - UNESCO Intangible Heritage)

history.origin: The Chewa migrated from the Congo Basin around the 15th century and established the powerful Maravi Empire that dominated the region.
history.colonialEra: British Nyasaland (1891-1964) imposed hut taxes and forced labor. The 1915 Chilembwe Uprising was a major resistance movement.
history.independence: Hastings Kamuzu Banda, a Chewa, led Malawi to independence in 1964 and ruled until 1994.

tribes.description: The Chewa are the largest ethnic group in Malawi, known for their matrilineal society, the secretive Gule Wamkulu masked dances (UNESCO heritage), and their reputation as 'The Warm Heart of Africa'.
